title: Bad branding doesn't kill a business. It taxes it.
fbPost:
Walked down my local street today and actually looked at the shopfronts.

Some of the branding was rough. Not charming rough. More 'someone's sister found clip art in 1998' rough.

And some of these places have been trading for years.

So here's the honest bit: a good logo won't save a bad business. But bad branding still costs you. It just sends the bill somewhere you can't see it.

The customer who felt something was off and walked past doesn't send a rejection email. You never meet them.

The fish and chip shop survives a bad logo because everyone knows what they're buying. A cosmetic clinic or a builder asking for a deposit? Different game entirely.
linkedinPost:
A bad logo won't kill a business. It taxes it, and the bill goes somewhere the owner never sees.

I walked my local street today and half the shopfronts had branding that looked like clip art with rent. Some have traded for years. So clearly it isn't fatal.

But look closer at who survives it. The fish and chip shop, the corner store, the known local with a low-risk, obvious purchase. Everything else is strong enough to carry a weak first impression.

Now change the ask. A cosmetic clinic wants a stranger to trust them near their face. A builder wants a renovation deposit before a nail goes in. A consultant wants premium rates. Those customers are already nervous, and a cheap-looking front door tells them to stay nervous.

The test was never whether the logo is pretty. It's whether your first impression matches the level of trust you're asking for. Good enough branding stays good enough right up until you go premium, go new, or chase a different customer. Then it quietly argues against everything else you've improved, and nobody tells you why.

title: Does a business need a polished logo and brand?
excerpt: A good logo won't save a bad business. But bad branding quietly taxes a good one. Here's when 'good enough' stops being good enough.
fbPost:
I walked the local shops today and some of the branding was rough. Not charming-rough. More like someone found clip art in 1998 and the printer was already smoking.

And yet some of these places have traded for years.

So here's the awkward truth: a good logo won't save a bad business. But bad branding quietly taxes a good one. It makes people hesitate, makes a premium price feel suspicious, and pulls in the wrong customers.

The fish and chip shop survives a dodgy logo because everyone knows what they're buying. A builder asking for a big deposit doesn't get the same pass.

The real question was never 'is my logo nice.' It's whether the first impression matches the trust you're asking for.
linkedinPost:
The question business owners ask about branding is almost always the wrong one. They ask whether the logo is nice. That conversation goes nowhere, because liking something is a matter of taste, and taste doesn't pay the rent.

The better question: does your first impression match the level of trust you're asking for?

A fish and chip shop survives a rough logo because the purchase is low-risk and familiar. Everyone already knows what they're buying. But a builder asking for a large renovation deposit is asking a stranger to hand over serious money before a single brick moves. A cosmetic clinic is asking someone to trust their face. When the ask is that high, looking like clip art with a lease becomes a real problem.

Here's the cost that never shows up in the accounts: the better customer who hesitates, feels something is off, and quietly walks past. They won't tell you it was the branding. They just choose the competitor who looked the part.

A good logo won't save a bad business. But bad branding will quietly tax a good one.

tldr.thesis: A good logo won't save a bad business. Bad branding will quietly tax a good one.
paragraphs: The question is never whether the logo is pretty. It's whether the first impression matches the level of trust you're asking for.
Charge more, win a new customer, or ask for a big deposit, and rough branding stops being harmless. People decide fast, and they walk past without telling you why.

formCta.heading: Wondering if your branding is holding you back?
formCta.lead: Tell us what your business does and what you're trying to charge for. We'll give you a straight answer on whether the brand is matching the ask.
formCta.legend: Where are you with your branding right now?

options.label: It looks rough and I know it
It's fine, but I want to charge more
I'm chasing a different kind of customer

formCta.textareaLabel: What does your business do, and what are you trying to win?
formCta.textareaPlaceholder: Anything from 'I want to charge premium prices but my shopfront looks dated' to 'people walk past and I don't know why'

title: The 3 Biggest Marketing Mistakes We See (And Why They're So Common)
excerpt: After working with dozens of businesses, the same patterns show up again and again. Not because people are lazy, but because no one has ever shown them what actually matters.

title: We're Building 30 Products in 30 Days (Because We Suck at Consistency)
excerpt: Two ADHD founders are building and shipping one product every day for 30 days starting 1 May 2026. It's a public challenge to prove we can stay consistent and an invitation to watch the work.

title: We Published 759 Pages in 10 Weeks. Here's What Showed Up.
excerpt: Ten weeks. 759 new pages, around 65 blog posts, 25 tools. The lesson isn't the volume. It's what volume forces a quiet business to stop pretending about.

title: AI Chatbots Are Transforming Business: What's Changed and What's Coming
excerpt: We wrote about business chatbots years ago. The landscape has changed completely. Here's the 2026 update: what the big players are doing, whether Claude and GPT have replaced everything, and what ManyChat's $140M raise means for small business.

title: AI Didn't Invent the Funnel. It Just Made the Old Bullshit Faster.
excerpt: I sat through an AI marketing webinar so you don't have to. The product was real. The pitch was a twenty-year-old sales script wearing a headset mic.

title: Your AI Skepticism Is Costing You Customers Right Now
excerpt: While you wait for AI to prove itself, your competitors are using it to respond faster, personalize better, and win the deals you're losing.

title: The AI Tools Playbook That Actually Ships Results
excerpt: Most small business owners tried ChatGPT once and quit. The AI Agents & Tools playbook shows you exactly which tools to use and how to wire them together for automated follow-ups, lead handling, and content generation.

title: Approve Every Invoice
excerpt: Some monthly fees make sense. The ones built on confusion don't. Why we'd rather be remembered than retained.

title: Our blogs are good. Our audience doesn’t read. So we fixed that.
excerpt: We wrote useful blogs for people who do not have time to read blogs. Slight oversight. So we fixed the format. Same post, same URL, now with a play button for the people who are in the ute.

title: Your Book Now button is losing people to uncertainty, not to a competitor.
excerpt: Day 26 of the 30-day build challenge: a free tool that drafts the five-line trust strip that sits above your booking button. Who this is for, what they get, what happens after, and a soft escape for the undecided.

title: Brandon told himself he didn't need a website for two years
excerpt: Brandon got quoted $5k+ for a website and spent two years telling himself he didn't need one. Then we built him something completely different.

title: Can you really trust Ian?
excerpt: Ian wrote a piece predicting AI's future. Then he compared it to human laziness and TikTok addiction. Let's talk about whether you should take him seriously.

title: Coming to us for a "website" is like going to a restaurant for "food"
excerpt: Day 5 of 30. I stopped building a demo for someone else and started building the tool we actually needed: a menu that lets you order the business asset you're hungry for, not just "a website."

title: Your contact form is collecting problems, not leads.
excerpt: Day 24 of the 30-day build challenge: a free tool that builds the three friendly questions you need in front of your contact form. Good-fit leads breeze through. Bad-fit leads get politely redirected.

title: DNS propagating. The two worst words in IT.
excerpt: A customer service agent killed a client's website and emails. Hours lost chasing DNS propagation. The thing got built anyway, but polish was not on the menu.

title: Most small business owners don't need more strategy. They need today to stop leaking.
excerpt: Day 22 of the 30-day build challenge: a free 7am card. Three inputs, one card. The thing to do, the thing to stop, the truth you're avoiding, the 20-minute version, the 2-hour version. Screenshot and get on with it.

title: Don't Lose Them at 404: How to Turn Errors into Engagement
excerpt: A custom 404 page is one of the most underrated tools in your digital arsenal. Here's why it matters for SEO and how to make one that actually works.

title: It's Easier To Pivot When You're Not Built Like A Shopping Centre
excerpt: AI isn't killing creativity. It's exposing business models that only worked while the client stayed confused. Weight is the real problem.

title: Fear Sells... Until It Doesn't: Why Scaring Your Audience is a Lazy Strategy
excerpt: Fear-based marketing gets clicks. It won't earn loyalty. Here's why scaring your audience is a shortcut that costs you trust, and what to do instead.

title: Filler Is the Leak After the Click
excerpt: Most homepages reach for the same few hundred words. Buyers have read them a thousand times. Day 19 of the 30-day challenge: a free scanner that scores your homepage for filler and tells you what a stranger actually reads.

title: Four Steps to Get Your Marketing Actually Working
excerpt: Most small business marketing fails because it's too complicated. Here's the simple four-step system that actually works.

title: Get a Brief, Not a Quote
excerpt: Most small business owners email agencies a vague 'can you quote us a website' and get ghosted. Day 20 of the 30-day challenge: a free intake tool that turns vague enquiries into a real brief, with an AI co-pilot that mirrors each answer back sharper.

title: Good marketing should make sense before it costs money.
excerpt: Most small business owners don't have a marketing problem. They have a clarity problem. PlainBlack helps you fix what actually matters, not just add to the noise.

title: The Hidden Cost of 'Winging It' in Your Marketing
excerpt: Winging it doesn't just waste time. It quietly costs you customers, momentum, and confidence every single day.

title: How to Fire Your Marketing Agency (And Why You Probably Should)
excerpt: Professional agencies charge $3-5k/month for processes you can learn in an afternoon. Here's how to take back control without burning bridges.

title: Ian Has Gone Off the Deep End. But Could This Actually Work?
excerpt: Lower the retirement age to 55. Give every Australian over 30 a universal basic income. Insane? Maybe. But the arguments against it are weaker than you'd think.

title: If your business runs on Claude, you don't own a business
excerpt: AI can run half your business now. But if the model you depend on changes overnight, what exactly do you own? Here's how to build on rented ground without losing the house.

title: If Your Marketing Feels Random, This Is Probably Why

title: Your local customers are not comparing your price. They are assessing your risk.
excerpt: Day 27 of the 30-day build challenge: a free tool that builds the trust section every local business website is missing. Headline, objections answered with proof, review spotlight, CTA. Six questions, thirty seconds.

title: More roofing leads are not better if half of them waste your quoting time

We're 10 days into a 30-day experiment to find out whether voice-led blogging tied to real work moves anything for a small business in 2026.

title: Your AI Doesn't Need More Prompts. It Needs Your Voice.

title: Your marketing may not need more content

title: Your Website Cost $8,000. It's Worth $50.

title: Why Most Small Business Websites Are Solving the Wrong Problem

title: You're Not Bad at Marketing. You're Just Guessing
